source: She had hardly arrived when the phone rang.
correct: Hardly had she arrived when the phone rang.
explanation: Inversion with <Hardly>: <Hardly> + auxiliary + subject + past participle + <when> + past simple.

prompt: Change to subjunctive with <It is essential that>
correct: It is essential that he attend the meeting.
explanation: Formal subjunctive after <It is essential/important/vital that>: subject + base verb (no conjugation).

prompt: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause
source: The man who is standing by the door is my uncle.
correct: The man standing by the door is my uncle.
explanation: Reduced relative clauses: remove <who is/that is> and keep the participle.

source: I didn't realize the truth until I read the report.
correct: Not until I read the report did I realize the truth.
explanation: <Not until> + clause + inverted auxiliary + subject for emphasis.

prompt: Change to passive with <It is said that>
source: People say that he is a genius.
correct: It is said that he is a genius.
He is said to be a genius.
hint: Two possible structures: <It is said that...> or <He is said to...>
explanation: Impersonal passive: <It is said/believed/reported that> or subject + <is said to> + infinitive.

prompt: Rewrite using a nominal clause with <what>
source: The thing that surprised me was his reaction.
correct: What surprised me was his reaction.
explanation: <What> replaces <The thing that> in nominal clauses for more natural style.

source: She understood the problem only after she read the full report.
correct: Only after she read the full report did she understand the problem.
Only after reading the full report did she understand the problem.

prompt: Change to a concessive clause with <much as>
source: I admire her talent, but I disagree with her methods.
correct: Much as I admire her talent, I disagree with her methods.
explanation: <Much as> + clause expresses concession: acknowledging something while contrasting.

source: Without your help, I would have failed.
correct: Were it not for your help, I would have failed.
explanation: <Were it not for> is a formal inversion of <If it were not for>.
Emphatic <do/does/did> + base verb adds emphasis to an affirmative statement.
